codeReview.summary: Clean, minimal change (36 lines) to tests/unit/test_cudf_backend.py. Adds a reusable WINDOWS_ONLY_WORKER_ISOLATION skipif marker and applies it to the two Windows-specific CUDA worker-isolation tests. Also adds a meta-test that verifies the skip guards are present and a monkeypatch-based unit test covering the non-Windows branch of _configure_cuda_worker_environment (returns device_id unchanged, preserves existing CUDA env vars). The implementation correctly matches the source function's behavior: on non-Windows, it early-returns device_id without touching os.environ. Two worker sessions were needed because the first could not merge from the isolated worktree; the second session only performed the merge and verified with pytest (470 passed, 14 skipped).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker deviated from skill procedure (TDD first, full local gates) because the feature description explicitly limited scope to merge-and-verify. Deviation was justified — the previous worker had already completed TDD and all gates on the feature branch. The skill's 'Final Step: Merge to Main' section was updated in a prior session with worktree merge instructions, which the second worker successfully followed.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.followedProcedure=false with two justified deviations: no failing test (implementation already done) and only ran pytest, not ruff/mypy (prior worker had already passed those).

sharedStateObservations.observation: The second worker skipped ruff and mypy after the merge, running only pytest. Since the change was a fast-forward merge of an already-gated commit, this is acceptable but not ideal — AGENTS.md requires all three gates before push.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff verification.commandsRun shows only 'uv run pytest -q' post-merge, not ruff or mypy. Feature description explicitly said 'run uv run pytest -q to confirm', so the worker followed instructions.

summary: Pass. The feature correctly adds Windows-only skip guards to two CUDA worker-isolation tests and adds coverage for the non-Windows code path in _configure_cuda_worker_environment. The implementation is minimal, correct, and well-structured. No code quality issues, no missing coverage, no security concerns. The two-session workflow (implement in worktree, then merge from primary checkout) completed successfully.
